actions.thought: I am on the button in a heads-up situation with a suited King (Ks 6s). This is a strong starting hand in this format. I will open with a standard raise to 300 to take control of the hand and build the pot early against the Big Blind.

In a Heads-Up match, the Button (SB) raising range is typically very wide. Holding A3o in the Big Blind, I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call (calling 200 to win 600 total). A3o has approximately 48-50% equity against a standard wide opening range, significantly higher than the 25% equity required to call. While out of position, the hand is too strong to fold. 3-betting is a viable polarized strategy, but flat calling is the standard, lower-variance line to keep the opponent's range wide and realize equity.

On a Flop of 2d Tc 8c, I hold Ac 3s. I have one overcard and a backdoor nut flush draw (Ac). As the pre-flop caller in the Big Blind, I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor. The standard line is to check my entire range here to the raiser, as the T-8-x texture connects reasonably well with a Button opening range. Leading (donk betting) would provide little utility, as it folds out bluffs and gets called by better hands. I will check and evaluate based on the opponent's action.

As the pre-flop aggressor in position, I will place a continuation bet on this board. While 2d-Tc-8c doesn't help my Ks-6s, I have the initiative and can represent a strong range. A bet of 300 (half the pot) is effective for applying pressure to the Big Blind's check and potentially winning the pot immediately.

I am facing a half-pot c-bet (300 into 600) on a 2d Tc 8c board holding Ac 3s. In a heads-up scenario, ranges are wide. I hold Ace-high which beats many of the opponent's pure bluffs (like QJ, K9, etc.) and has 6 outs to top pair (Aces and Threes), plus the Ace of Clubs gives me a backdoor nut flush draw. To call 300 to win a pot that will be 1200, I need 25% equity. My hand has sufficient equity and playability to continue for one street. Folding is too weak given the heads-up dynamic and my specific holding (overcard + BDFD). Raising turns my hand into a bluff unnecessarily. Calling is the standard defensive action to realize equity.

On the Turn, the board is 2d Tc 8c 3h. The 3h improves my hand significantly, giving me a pair of Threes (bottom pair) with a top kicker (Ace). In a heads-up match, this hand has decent showdown value. As the pre-flop caller playing out of position, the standard approach is to check to the aggressor. Leading out (donk betting) here is suboptimal because it folds out the opponent's air (which I am now beating) and isolates me against better hands like Tx, 8x, or overpairs. By checking, I induce the opponent to either bluff with missed overcards/draws or check back, allowing me to see a river or call a reasonable bet with my pair.

The turn card (3h) is a blank and doesn't improve my hand. I have King-high with no draws on a board that contains a flush draw and some straight possibilities. Pro has checked to me after calling my flop bet. Since I have some showdown value with King-high and no equity to continue semi-bluffing effectively against a range that likely called the flop with a piece of the board or a draw, checking back is the most sensible play. It allows me to see a free river and control the size of the pot.

I hold a pair of Threes with an Ace kicker on a board of 2d Tc 8c 3h Qd. The opponent c-bet the flop but checked back the turn, which caps their range and suggests they might be on a draw or hold a marginal made hand. The River Queen is an overcard that might improve some of the opponent's floats (like QJ), but missed club draws and straight draws (like J9 or 97) are still in their range. Betting out (donking) achieves little, as worse hands will fold and better hands will call. By checking, I allow the opponent to either bluff with their missed draws (which I beat) or check back with a weaker hand like Ace-high or King-high. My hand has sufficient showdown value to try and win the pot cheaply, but not enough to bet for value.

The river Queen is an overcard that doesn't help my King-high. My opponent has checked the turn and river. While I could attempt a bluff to represent hitting the Queen or a Ten, I have some showdown value against missed club draws (like J-high or lower) that might have called the flop. Checking back allows me to see the showdown without risking more chips in a spot where the opponent's flop-calling range, which likely includes pairs like Tens or Eights, might not fold to a single river bet. In the first hand of the tournament, I prefer the lower-variance play of taking the free showdown.

I hold pocket Sixes (6d, 6c) on the Button in a heads-up match. This is a strong starting hand relative to a random hand range. Being in position, I want to take the initiative. Raising serves to build the pot with a likely best hand and denies the Big Blind free equity. A standard min-raise to 200 is appropriate given the stack depth (over 90 BBs), allowing me to play aggressively while keeping the pot manageable if 3-bet.

I am in the Big Blind with 9s 8h. The opponent on the Button has raised to 200, which is a standard opening size. Given that I need to call 100 more to win a pot of 300, I am getting 3:1 odds. 98 offsuit is a solid hand to defend in a heads-up situation against a wide button range. I will call to see a flop.

I missed the flop completely with 9-high. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check and see how the opponent reacts. There is no reason to lead out with a bet here.

The flop falls 4h Jh 4c, pairing the board. I hold pocket Sixes (6d 6c). While I have a made hand (two pair), it is a marginal one. I am behind any Jack (Jx) and obviously any 4. I am ahead of flush draws, lower pocket pairs, and unmatched overcards. Betting here creates a 'way ahead / way behind' situation where I likely only get action from better hands or strong draws, and fold out hands I dominate. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and potentially induce bluffs from Flash on later streets or get value from Ace-highs that might peel a turn card if checked to. On this paired texture, protecting against overcards is less critical than avoiding bloating the pot against a Jack. Therefore, checking is the most prudent play.

I have 9-high on a board of 4h-Jh-4c-Qs. The flop went check-check after I defended the big blind pre-flop. The Queen on the turn is generally better for the pre-flop raiser's range. Since I have no showdown value and no draws, I will check again to see if my opponent checks behind or if I can reach a cheap showdown/identify where I stand. I'm not looking to bluff this specific card against a professional who might have picked up a pair or is slow-playing.

On the turn, the board reads 4h Jh 4c Qs. The Queen is a scare card that connects well with the opponent's Big Blind defending range (Qx) and adds a second overcard to my pocket Sixes. After I checked back the flop, my range is somewhat capped, though the Queen hits some of my float/check-back range. However, my specific hand (66) has now dropped significantly in relative strength. I am behind any Queen, Jack, Four, or higher pocket pair. Betting achieves little: worse hands (like A-high or small pairs) likely fold, and better hands will call or raise. Checking back allows me to get to showdown cheaply with my marginal made hand, hoping to beat missed draws or Ace-highs on the river, while avoiding bloating the pot in a spot where I am frequently behind.

I have improved to a pair of eights on the river. Given the passive action on the flop and turn (check-check), it is unlikely my opponent holds a strong hand like a Jack or a Queen, as those would typically bet for value or protection earlier. However, a pair of eights is still relatively weak showdown value. By checking, I allow my opponent to potentially bluff with air or check back weaker hands, maximizing my chance of winning the pot without risking a raise from a trapped hand.

I hold pocket Sixes (6d, 6c) on a board of 4h Jh 4c Qs 8d. The pot is small (400) as both players have checked the flop and turn. The river card (8d) is not particularly favorable, as it pairs random 8x hands and is higher than my pair. My hand has showdown value against missed heart draws, Ace-highs, and lower pocket pairs (22, 33, 55). If I bet, worse hands like Ace-high will likely fold, and better hands (8x, Jx, Qx, 77+) will call. Thus, betting serves no value purpose and turns my hand into a bluff unnecessarily. Checking back allows me to realize my equity and attempt to win at showdown against the bottom of the opponent's range without risking more chips.

I am on the button with Ace-Seven offsuit. In heads-up play, any Ace is a premium starting hand. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and take the initiative, consistent with my sizing in previous hands.

I am in the Big Blind with 8c 5h facing a 3x open (300 chips) from the Button. My opponent has effectively put in 200 more for me to call. The pot odds are 200 to win 600 (33%), but 85o is a very weak hand (bottom 15-20% of hands). Being out of position with a disconnected offsuit hand against a larger-than-minimum raise makes equity realization difficult. Defending here is likely -EV compared to folding. I will wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind (Button) with 9d 2d. While suited hands have some potential, 9-2 is unconnected and has very low high-card value, placing it near the bottom of the starting hand range. Even in Heads-Up play where ranges are wide, 92s is typically a fold deep-stacked (100BB). Opening invites 3-bets or puts me in a spot where I am easily dominated post-flop. To minimize chip loss and variance early in the match against an opponent showing aggression, I will fold.

Holding a strong pocket pair like Nines heads-up, I have a significant equity advantage over the Big Blind's range. I will raise to 300 to build the pot and exert pressure from the button, which is a standard opening size in this structure.

I hold Pocket Twos (2s 2c) in the Big Blind. My opponent (Flash) has raised to 300 (3BB). In heads-up play, any pocket pair has sufficient equity to defend against an open raise. The price to call is 200 chips into a pot that will be 600, requiring 33% equity, which 22 has against a standard opening range. I am out of position post-flop, so calling is superior to 3-betting, which would bloat the pot with a hand that is difficult to navigate if I don't flop a set.

I hold Pocket Twos on a board of [5s 5c Ad]. This is a very favorable board for the pre-flop raiser (Flash), who has all strong Aces in their range. My hand has very little showdown value and drawing potential is negligible (2 outs to a set). Leading out serves no purpose as I fold out bluffs and get action only from better hands. The standard line is to check to the aggressor. If faced with a bet, I will likely have to fold, but for now, the correct action is to check.

The board shows 5s-5c-Ad. My pocket Nines are a strong hand, but the Ace is a likely card in the Big Blind's range. Since the board is quite dry and the opponent checked, I will check back to control the pot size and keep their bluffs in. This also prevents me from being check-raised off a hand that still has significant showdown value.

I hold Pocket Twos on a board of 5s 5c Ad 6s. My hand is currently extremely weak, beating only missed high card hands. My opponent checked back the flop, which could imply a weak Ace, a pocket pair, a slow-played monster, or total air. The turn 6s doesn't help me, and arguably hits some of the opponent's range (like 6x or spade draws). Betting here turns my hand into a bluff, but it is unlikely to fold out an Ace or a 6, and only folds out hands I am currently beating or splitting with (like K-high). Checking allows me to potentially see a cheap showdown or fold if faced with aggression, as my hand cannot withstand significant pressure.

After checking the flop, the 6s turn doesn't provide many reasons to change course. Pocket nines are strong in a vacuum but marginal on this board. Pro's second check indicates weakness or a trap. By checking back, I maintain the pot size, hide the strength of my hand, and prepare for a showdown or a river decision. This avoids being forced out of the pot by a check-raise from a better hand while realizing equity against their entire range.

I hold Pocket Twos (2s 2c) on a board of 5s 5c Ad 6s 8h. The action has gone check-check on both the flop and turn. My hand constitutes Two Pair (Fives and Twos) with an Ace kicker, which beats the board and any unpaired hand (like K-high or Q-high) my opponent might have. The opponent's double check-back line suggests either a weak made hand (like a weak Ace, mid-pair, or 6x) attempting to get to showdown cheaply, or complete air that gave up on the Ace-high board. Betting here achieves little: worse hands (air) will fold, and better hands (any higher pocket pair, 6x, 8x, or Ace) will likely call. Therefore, betting serves neither for value nor effectively as a bluff. My best strategy is to check and hope to show down against a high-card hand. If the opponent bets, I will fold, as I only beat a bluff.

I am holding pocket Nines on a board of 5s-5c-Ad-6s-8h. Since I checked back on both the flop and the turn, I have successfully controlled the pot and kept my hand's strength disguised. Pro has checked for the third time on the river. While it is highly probable that my Nines are the best hand, betting for value is risky; an Ace or a Five in Pro's range might be waiting to check-call or check-raise, and it is unlikely that a worse hand will call a bet. Checking back allows me to realize my showdown value and win the pot against Pro's missed range without facing any further aggression.

In 100bb Heads-Up play, 97o is a marginal hand that sits on the boundary of playability. Folding is a reasonable option to reduce variance, but folding too many buttons allows the opponent to auto-profit. Given I folded my previous button (92d), I want to engage here. Raising to 200 bloats the pot with a weak hand that plays poorly against a calling range. Limping (completing the blind) is a standard play for this hand class, allowing me to see a flop cheaply in position while getting 3:1 direct pot odds on the call. If the opponent raises, I can easily fold. If they check, I can use my position to realize equity or steal small pots.

I am in the Big Blind with a very weak hand, 6h 2d. The opponent (Pro) has only completed the small blind, allowing me to see a flop for free by checking. There is no strategic advantage to raising with such a weak holding out of position, so I will take the free card.

I have flopped bottom pair (Deuces) on a paired board of Aces. While this gives me two pair (Aces and Deuces), it is a relatively weak hand in the context of this board. Since I am out of position against the Button (Pro) in a limped pot, checking is the most prudent play to control the pot and evaluate the opponent's reaction. Betting would likely only be called by an Ace, a better pair, or a flush draw, so I will check to see if I can get to a cheap showdown or catch a bluff.

I hold 97o on a paired board of [Ah Ac 2h]. This board texture hits the Big Blind's checking range reasonably well (lots of random Ax), and while it is hard to connect with, my hand has almost zero equity if called. Betting turns my hand into a pure bluff immediately, and I likely only fold out hands that are currently beating me but are still weak (like King-high or Queen-high). Given the passive history in previous hands (lots of check-downs), checking back allows me to realize my positional advantage, potentially hit a pair on the turn, or evaluate whether to bluff later streets if the opponent shows continued weakness. There is no need to bloat the pot with 9-high.

The turn card is the Ten of Diamonds. My hand remains two pair, Aces and Deuces. Given that the flop was checked around in a limped pot, it is unlikely the opponent has a strong Ace. However, the Ten of Diamonds is a card that could have helped my opponent's range if they were holding a Ten. Since my hand is relatively weak and has some showdown value, I will check again to keep the pot small and potentially induce a bluff or see a cheap river.

We are on the turn with 9-high on an [Ah Ac 2h Td] board in a limped pot. The opponent has checked to me twice. The arrival of the Ten connects with some of the opponent's random broadway checking range. Given the opponent's passive history (evidenced by multiple check-downs in previous hands) and the tendency for players to call light on paired boards in limped pots (sticky with any pair like 2x or Tx), a bluff here has low success probability and high risk. I have no value to bet. Checking back allows me to see a free river, potentially pairing my 7 or 9 to win, or minimizing loss against better high-card hands.

The board has paired Aces and includes a Queen and a Ten. I have bottom pair (Deuces), giving me two pair: Aces and Deuces. The pot was limped pre-flop and checked through the flop and turn. My hand has some showdown value in this scenario, but it is very weak relative to the board's overcards. Betting is unlikely to get called by a worse hand or fold out a better one. Checking is the best option to reach showdown for free in this small pot.

I arrive at the river with 9-high in a limped pot. The board has run out with broadway cards (Ten, Queen) alongside the paired Aces, which hits the Big Blind's random checking range reasonably well (e.g., Qx, Tx, or random pocket pairs). While the opponent has shown weakness by checking three streets, the pot is very small (2 BBs). Bluffing here to fold out King-high or Jack-high is possible, but risky given the opponent's passive history and the likelihood they have connected with the late street cards or hold a sticky small pair. I have no showdown value, but saving chips is superior to firing a low-EV bluff into a range that likely calls. I will check back and concede the pot.

Holding 5c 4c on the button, I have a strong speculative hand with good post-flop playability. I've consistently raised to 300 in previous hands from this position to maintain aggression and apply pressure to the big blind. A raise here is standard.

I hold 92o in the Big Blind, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. My opponent, Flash, has raised to 300 (3BB). While I'm getting 2:1 pot odds, my equity against a standard opening range is significantly lower than the required 33%, and I will be out of position. Defending with 92o against a 3x raise is -EV. The correct play is to fold and wait for a better hand.

I hold 8d3d in the Small Blind. This is a very weak hand (bottom 15-20 percentile). Although it is suited, the gap is large and the high card is low. Reviewing previous hands, I folded 9d2d in a similar spot (Hand #4), and my opponent 'Flash' has shown aggression pre-flop when on the button. While they checked back my limp in Hand #6, this hand is too weak to profitably defend against a potential isolation raise or play out of position post-flop without hitting hard. With 100BB stacks, preserving chips is better than engaging with trash hands. Folding is the standard play.

I have Pocket Kings, the second-strongest starting hand. My standard opening raise size from the button in this tournament has been 300, and I will maintain that consistency here to build the pot while keeping my range disguised.

I am in the Big Blind with 9d 2h, facing a 3bb raise (300) from the Button. This is statistically one of the weakest hands in heads-up hold'em. Although I am getting 2:1 pot odds requiring ~33% equity, 92o realizes its equity very poorly out of position. Against a 3x size, this is a standard fold. I folded a similar hand (2c 9h) in Hand #7 against the same action. Preserving chips is the correct play.

I am on the Button with 7h 5h, a suited one-gapper with decent playability in heads-up poker. Having folded my last two buttons (Hands 4 and 8) and limped one prior (Hand 6), raising now helps balance my range and take initiative. A minimum raise to 200 is a standard size that allows me to play a pot in position without overcommitting with a speculative hand, while still having enough equity to continue against a call.

In a heads-up situation, defending the Big Blind against a min-raise from the Small Blind is standard with a wide range of hands. Ten-Seven offsuit has decent equity and I am getting 3:1 odds to see a flop. Folding would be too tight, and raising is unnecessary given the strength of the hand.

On a monotone flop of 4s-Ks-9s, I have the Ten of Spades, giving me a flush draw. As the Big Blind, I will check to the pre-flop aggressor to see their reaction to this board texture. This allows me to control the pot size while holding a draw that has decent equity but is not the nuts.

I hold 7h 5h on a monotone flop of 4s Ks 9s. I have no pair, no draw, and no spade in my hand. This board texture interacts heavily with the Big Blind's calling range, which contains many suited hands and offsuit hands holding a single spade. With zero equity and very low fold equity on such a wet board, betting as a pure bluff is negative EV. The opponent has shown passive tendencies, often checking down, so checking back preserves my stack and allows me to see a free turn card, potentially hitting a pair or evaluating later streets.

I have improved to a pair of Tens on the turn. Given that the pre-flop aggressor checked back on a very wet flop, it is unlikely they hold a King or a made flush. I will check again to control the pot and potentially induce a bluff from missed draws or weaker hands. My Ten of Spades also provides a redraw to a flush, adding to my hand's strength.

On the turn, the board is 4s Ks 9s Td. I hold 7h 5h, giving me 7-high with no draw and no spades. The Ten of Diamonds improves the board for hands like QJ (straight) or Tx, and the monotone texture remains dangerous. Having checked the flop, starting a bluff now with zero equity is ill-advised against a passive opponent who may well be holding a spade or a pair. The most prudent play is to check back, preserving chips and giving up on this pot unless the river presents a very specific opportunity.

On the river, the fourth spade (Jack of Spades) completes a potential flush. I hold the Ten of Spades, giving me a 10-high flush. While this is a very strong hand in most contexts, a four-flush board is extremely dangerous as any player holding a single spade higher than the Ten (the Ace or Queen of Spades) beats me. Given that the pre-flop aggressor has checked both the flop and the turn, their range is wide. By checking, I control the pot size and can safely see a showdown if they check back, or evaluate their bet size if they choose to represent a higher flush or a bluff.

The river brings the Jack of Spades, putting four spades on the board (Ks 9s 4s Js) and a possible straight. I hold 7h 5h, leaving me with King-high and a 7 kicker. My hand has practically zero showdown value, beating only exactly 3-2 without a spade. The opponent has been passive throughout the tournament, often checking down hands. While a bet here could represent the Ace or Queen of Spades, it is risky against a passive player who might call with any small spade or two pair. Since the pot is small (400 chips) and I have shown no strength on previous streets, bluffing into a 4-flush board is likely negative EV. I will check back and surrender the pot.

In heads-up play, maintaining a high opening frequency from the button is standard strategy. This hand, Six-Deuce of hearts, is suited and offers some post-flop playability. More importantly, my opponent (Pro) has folded to a 3x opening raise in 60% of our previous encounters (Hands 3, 7, and 9). Given this observed fold equity, raising here is likely to be immediately profitable and continues to put pressure on a relatively tight opponent. If called, I still have roughly 40% equity against a random hand.

The opponent, Flash, has raised to 300 (3x BB) from the button in nearly every recorded instance (Hands 1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11), indicating an extremely wide, possibly 100%, opening range. Against a 100% range, J6o holds approximately 53% equity. I am being offered 2:1 pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600), requiring only 33% equity to break even raw. Although playing out of position is a disadvantage that reduces realized equity, the equity edge of J6o against a 100% range combined with the pot odds makes this a mathematically correct defense. Folding too frequently to these 3x opens would allow the opponent to profitably steal the blinds with any two cards. Additionally, the opponent has shown some passivity post-flop (e.g., checking back Hand 5), which may allow me to realize equity more easily.

I hold middle pair (6s) on a 9s-6s-4d board. My opponent, Flash, has shown a tendency to open very wide (3x raises) but has checked back post-flop in several previous hands. As the pre-flop caller out of position, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. My hand has decent showdown value against a wide range, but betting out (donking) would likely fold out worse hands and isolate me against better ones. I will check and intend to call a standard continuation bet, as middle pair is too strong to fold immediately given the opponent's wide pre-flop range and the board texture.

I have flopped middle pair on a semi-coordinated board (9s-6s-4d). My opponent checked to me, which is consistent with the passive play they have shown in previous hands. A continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) is appropriate here to protect my equity against overcards and draws, while also potentially ending the hand immediately. If I am called, I can evaluate the turn, but given the board texture and my middle pair, maintaining aggression while I likely have the best hand is the preferred line.

I hold middle pair (Sixes) on a 9s-6s-4d board against an opponent who opens nearly 100% of buttons and has fired a half-pot continuation bet (300 into 600). I am getting 3:1 pot odds (calling 300 to win a total pot of 1200), which requires approximately 25% equity to call profitably. Given the opponent's extremely wide pre-flop range, my middle pair is well ahead of their random air and draws, and competitive against some of their value range. Raising would likely fold out the bluffs I beat and only get action from better hands (top pair, overpairs), whereas folding is incorrect given my hand strength and the price. Calling keeps their range wide and allows me to re-evaluate on the turn.

I have improved to two pair (Jacks and Sixes) on the turn, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. As the out-of-position player against the pre-flop aggressor who also c-bet the flop, the standard line is to check. This gives the opponent an opportunity to continue bluffing with draws or air, or to value bet a worse hand (like a single Jack or 9). Leading out (donking) would likely cause him to fold his bluffs immediately, minimizing my potential gain. I intend to check-raise or call if he bets, but checking is the first step to maximize value.

The Jack of clubs on the turn is an overcard that potentially improves Pro's range if they were calling the flop with hands like JT or QJ. My pair of sixes retains some showdown value, but it is unlikely to be called by worse hands if I bet, and better hands like a 9 or a J certainly won't fold. By checking back, I control the pot size and ensure I see the river for free, which is the most prudent play with a marginal hand in this spot.

I hold top two pair (Jacks and Sixes) on a board of 4d-9s-6s-Jc-5d. My opponent, Flash, plays an extremely wide range pre-flop and has shown a tendency to check back turns and rivers (e.g., Hand #1, #5, #10), indicating he often takes showdown lines with marginal hands rather than value betting thin. His check on the Turn (Jc) strongly suggests he does not have a monster hand (like a set or strong two pair), as he likely would have bet to protect against the flush and straight draws on the board. The River 5d completes the 7-8 straight, which is a plausible hand for him (open-ended on the flop/turn, checking for a free card), but it represents a small portion of his wide range. If I check, he likely checks back his one-pair hands (Jx, 9x), denying me value. By betting, I target those hands. A bet of 500 (approx. 40% pot) is small enough to induce a call from a Jack or potentially a Nine, while limiting my loss if he raises with a straight.

Pro's lead on the river for 500 into a 1200 pot after I checked back the turn is a significant deviation from their previously passive play. While my pair of sixes has some showdown value, the river five is a dangerous card that completes straights for hands like 78 and 23, both of which are well within Pro's pre-flop and flop calling range. Additionally, Pro could easily be value betting a 9 or a Jack. Given Pro's tendency to check-down marginal hands in previous pots, this lead-out bet likely represents a made hand that beats a weak pair of sixes. Folding here allows me to preserve my chip lead and avoid a potentially losing call against a range that is heavily weighted toward value.

I hold Queen-Ten offsuit (QTo) on the Button. In heads-up play, this is a strong starting hand that should be played for value. My opponent 'Flash' has shown a tendency to defend their Big Blind against min-raises, but playing in position with a broadway hand gives me an advantage. I will open with a standard min-raise to 200 chips to build the pot and seize the initiative.

I am in the Big Blind with 8d 2s, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. The opponent (Pro) has raised to 200 from the Small Blind. While I only need to call 100 more to see a flop, the hand has very poor playability and equity realization, especially out of position. Defending such a weak range can lead to significant losses in the post-flop stages. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 8h 4c. This is a very weak hand, but it is a heads-up situation and I have position. However, looking at the previous hands, Pro has been playing relatively solid and I have already seen some folds to my pre-flop raises. 84offsuit is generally not worth playing even in a steal attempt from the small blind against a competent opponent. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my position and stack.

I am in the Small Blind (Button) with K6o. In heads-up play, a King-high hand is reasonably strong and is a standard open-raise. I have raised to 200 in previous hands from this position (e.g., Hand #12, #10), and the opponent has folded occasionally. Raising to 200 (min-raise) allows me to take the initiative, utilize position post-flop if called, and potentially win the pot immediately if the opponent folds weak holdings. There is no reason to deviate from the aggressive strategy established.

I am in the Big Blind with 7d4d, a suited one-gapper. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 200, which means I only need to call 100 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 400. This 3-to-1 price is generally acceptable with suited connectors or suited gappers in a heads-up situation, as it allows me to realize my equity and potentially outplay the opponent post-flop or hit a strong draw/hand.

I have flopped middle pair (fours) with a 7 kicker on a relatively low board of 6-4-3. In heads-up play, middle pair is a decent hand, but as the Big Blind acting first on the flop after calling a pre-flop raise, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. This allows me to gauge the strength of Pro's hand based on their betting action and potentially keep the pot small with a marginal hand. I will check and likely call a standard-sized continuation bet to see how the turn develops.

I have AKo, which is a premium hand, especially in a heads-up situation. I am on the button and have a stack advantage. I hold 6d 7d (suited connectors) in the Big Blind facing a 3x open (300) from the Button. The pot odds are 200 to win 400, requiring 33% equity. Given the deep stacks (90+ BB) and the playability of the hand, defending by calling is the standard and profitable play to realize equity and navigate post-flop.

I hold 6d 7d on a Jc Td 4c board. I have no pair and no immediate draws, only backdoor straight and flush possibilities. I hold Ace-King high with two overcards and the backdoor nut flush draw (Ac). The flop of Jc-Td-4c is somewhat connected, but as the pre-flop aggressor, I maintain a range advantage. I hold 6d 7d on a Jc Td 4c board. The board texture heavily favors the opponent's pre-flop raising range, which includes many broadway hands containing Jacks or Tens. Facing a half-pot c-bet of 300, my equity is negligible. Sticking to my plan from the start of the street, I must fold to this aggression.

I am on the Button (SB) with Kc6c, a suited King. In heads-up play, this is a strong starting hand, well above the median. I have pocket tens (Tc, Ts), which is a premium starting hand in heads-up play. The opponent opened from the button for 200. I will 3-bet to 600 total to build the pot for value and take initiative. This sizing is standard and puts pressure on a wide button opening range while allowing me to extract value from weaker pairs or high cards that might call.

I opened to 200 with K6s from the Button. My opponent, Flash, 3-bet to 600. I am on the button with Qs 3d. In heads-up play, maintaining positional aggression is key. I have consistently raised to 300 from the button in previous hands to put pressure on Pro. While Q3 offsuit is a marginal hand, the fold equity and the advantage of being in position for the rest of the hand make a standard raise the preferred play to stay consistent with my established table image.

The opponent 'Flash' has been opening frequently from the button with a consistent 3x sizing (300 chips). Against this wide opening range, Ace-Two offsuit (A2o) holds sufficient equity to defend the Big Blind, especially given the pot odds (calling 200 to win a pot that will be 600, requiring 33% equity). While A2o is tricky to play out of position, folding an Ace heads-up against a wide range is exploitable. I defended A3o in a similar spot in Hand #1. Calling keeps the pot manageable and allows me to realize equity when I pair up.

I hold A2o on a Qh 7s 6c board. This is a fairly dry board but it connects reasonably well with a typical button opening range. I missed the flop completely, holding only Ace-high. My plan is to check and likely fold to a continuation bet, as my hand is weak and the opponent has shown a high frequency of c-betting in previous hands (e.g., Hand #1, #11, #21, #25).

I have flopped top pair with my Queen. While my kicker (3) is weak, in a heads-up situation, top pair is a very strong hand. Pro checked, so I will place a standard continuation bet of 300 chips (half the pot) to extract value from weaker hands like middle pairs or straight draws and to protect my hand.

I hold Ace-Two offsuit on a Queen-Seven-Six rainbow board. I checked to the pre-flop aggressor, and Flash has continued with a bet of 300 (half-pot). Flash has demonstrated a high frequency of continuation betting in previous hands. While I technically have Ace-high, I have no flush draw and only very remote backdoor straight possibilities. The board texture connects well with the Button's range (Qx, 7x, 6x, straight draws), making it likely I am behind or will face further aggression. Folding is the correct decision to preserve chips.

I hold a premium hand, AKs (King of Clubs, Ace of Clubs), on the Button. This is one of the strongest starting hands in heads-up play. My standard opening raise size has been 2 min-raises (200), which allows me to build the pot while keeping the opponent's calling range wide. I want to extract value from weaker Ax, Kx, and other holdings. Position will be advantageous post-flop. Raising to 200 is the most +EV play here to start building a pot.

I am in the Big Blind with Jd8h. The opponent (Pro) has raised to 200 from the Small Blind/Button. I need to call 100 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 400. This is a standard defense with a hand that has some playability, especially given the pot odds (3:1) and being in position for the rest of the hand isn't possible, but I am closing the action pre-flop. I have a healthy stack and can afford to see a flop here.

I have hit middle pair (8s) on an Ace-high board. As the Big Blind checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard play. I have a hand with decent showdown value and will look to check-call a standard continuation bet, but leading out here would likely only be called by an Ace or a better 8, while folding out hands I currently beat.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (AK) on a very dry, rainbow Ace-high board (A-8-5). Given that I hold an Ace and a King, I significantly block the hands that would call a bet (worse Ax). My opponent is likely to have air or a weak underpair. Betting now would likely fold out his air, whereas checking back allows him to either bluff on the turn or catch a piece of the board to call a value bet later. This line also protects my checking range on Ace-high boards, consistent with my play in previous hands (e.g., Hand #20 and #24 where I checked back A-high flops). Protection is not a concern on this texture.

I currently hold third pair (8s) on a board of As-8c-5d-Td. Pro, the pre-flop aggressor, checked back on the flop, which suggests they likely do not have a strong Ace or are playing a marginal hand passively. The Ten of Diamonds on the turn is a potential improve for some of their range, but my hand still has decent showdown value. I will check here to see if Pro intends to bluff or if they will check behind again, allowing me to reach the river cheaply and realize my equity without bloating the pot with a marginal holding.

I checked the flop with Top Pair Top Kicker (AK) to disguise my hand strength and protect my checking range. The Turn card (Td) brings a second flush draw and connects with the board (potential straight draws like J9 or gutshots). Since my opponent checked again, it is likely they do not have a strong Ace or two pair, but they might have picked up a pair of Tens or a draw. I should now bet to extract value from these weaker hands (Tx, 8x, draws) and to deny free equity realization to flush/straight draws. Checking back twice risks giving a free river card on a board that is becoming wetter. A half-pot bet of 200 is appropriate to get called by worse hands.

On the turn, the board is As-8c-5d-Td. I have middle pair (8s) with a Jack kicker. My opponent (Pro) checked behind on the flop, which often indicates they do not have a strong Ace. When Pro bets 200 into 400 on the turn after checking the flop, it looks like a delayed continuation bet or a thin value bet with a marginal hand like a Ten, or perhaps a draw. Given the pot odds (calling 200 to win 600), I have sufficient equity with middle pair to call one street and see what happens on the river, especially since their line doesn't strongly represent a monster.

I have middle pair (8s) on a board that contains an Ace and a Ten. My opponent checked the flop and bet the turn, which I called. The river is a blank 3h. At this point, my hand has showdown value but is unlikely to be the best if I face significant aggression. Leading out here doesn't make much sense as I won't get called by worse hands and only better hands will continue. Checking is the standard play to see if I can get to a cheap showdown or catch a bluff.

I hold Top Pair Top Kicker (AK) on a board of A-8-5-T-3. After checking the flop to disguise my strength and betting the turn for value when checked to, the river brings a blank (3h). The opponent checked again. His range likely consists of weaker Aces (Ax) that checked-called the turn, or perhaps a pair of Tens (Tx) that is sticky. Given the pot size of 800, a value bet is the clear play to extract chips from these worse hands. Checking back misses value. A bet of 400 (half-pot) targets his weak Aces while keeping his calling range reasonably wide enough to include some second pairs or curious calls, while not being so large that only two-pair or better calls.

I hold middle pair (Eights) on a board with an Ace and a Ten. Pro (the opponent) checked the flop but has bet both the turn and the river. Given the pot odds of 3:1 (calling 400 to win 1200), my hand serves as a decent bluff catcher against missed draws or lower pocket pairs that the opponent might be betting for thin value or as a bluff. I am on the button with a pocket pair (2d 2c). In heads-up play, any pocket pair is a strong starting hand. Opponent raises to 300 (3bb) from the button. I am in the Big Blind with A6o. The opponent has been opening frequently (3bb size), suggesting a wide range. The flop comes Q-9-4 rainbow, which is a fairly dry board but connects with the pre-flop raiser's broadway range. I hold Ace-high with no significant draws. I am the pre-flop aggressor with pocket deuces. The flop of 4h-Qc-9s contains two overcards to my pair. The opponent has raised pre-flop and continued with a standard 300 chip c-bet (half pot) on a Q-9-4 rainbow flop. Folding is the prudent decision consistent with my previous play.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with K5o. In heads-up play, this hand is strong enough to open-raise for value and to steal the blinds. I have been dealt a premium hand, AdKd, on the button. I hold KcQs, a very strong hand in heads-up play (top 5-10% of hands). My opponent Flash has raised to 300 (3BB), consistent with his previous opening sizing. I hold Top Pair with a strong kicker (KQ) on a Kh-6h-7c board. My opponent, Flash, has shown a high frequency of continuation betting in previous hands (e.g., Hands 1, 15, 19, 21, 23, 25, 27, 29). I have top pair, top kicker on a draw-heavy board (Kh 6h 7c). I need to bet for value against weaker Kings, and protection against heart draws or straight draws. A half-pot bet of 300 is consistent with my previous sizing and allows me to build the pot against Pro's calling range.

I hold Top Pair with a strong kicker (KQ) on a Kh-6h-7c board. I hold Top Pair with a strong kicker (KQ) on a board that has become wetter with the Turn card (Th), putting three hearts on the board [Kh 6h 7c Th]. The Ten of Hearts is a significant scare card as it completes potential flushes and straights (like 8-9). It prevents me from being check-raised off my hand and allows me to evaluate the river situation. I hold Top Pair with a strong kicker (KQ) on a board of [Kh 6h 7c Th 2s]. My opponent, Flash, bet the flop but checked back the turn. If I bet, I likely fold out the hands I beat (Tens, underpairs, missed draws) and only get called by Kings, effectively chopping or winning a small amount, while risking a raise. By checking, I can induce bluffs from his missed draws or allow him to bet a worse King for value. Given his history of aggression when sensed weakness (e.g., Hand 19), checking is the most profitable line to extract value from bluffs or get to showdown cheaply.

I have Top Pair with the best possible kicker (TPTK). The river Deuce of Spades is a complete brick that doesn't change the board texture. I hold Top Pair with a strong kicker (KQ) on a completed board of [Kh 6h 7c Th 2s]. The pot is 1200. After I checked the river, Flash bet 600. Folding Top Pair here would be too weak. Raising would likely only get action from better hands. Calling is the optimal play.

I am on the Button with 9c7c, a suited one-gapper which is a strong speculative hand in heads-up play. I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Eight offsuit. Pro has raised from the Button to 200. Given the 3:1 pot odds (100 to call into a total pot of 300) and the fact that J8o is a standard defending hand in heads-up play, I will call to see the flop.

I have middle pair on a relatively wet board (King-high with straight and flush possibilities). My hand has some showdown value but isn't strong enough to lead out into the pre-flop raiser.

I hold middle pair (Nines) with a weak kicker on a fairly wet board (King-Eight-Nine with two spades). I do not want to face a check-raise, which would force me to fold a hand with reasonable showdown value.

I have middle pair (Eights). The flop went check-check, which suggests Pro might not have a strong King or is playing cautiously. The 3 on the turn doesn't change much. I hold middle pair (Nines) on a Kc 8s 9s 3d board. The 3d is a brick that doesn't change the board texture. Furthermore, betting opens me up to a check-raise which would be difficult to call. Checking back allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and either induce bluffs on the river or get to showdown cheaply.

The river is a 2d, and the board texture remains relatively unchanged. I have middle pair (Eights) with a Jack kicker. There is little value in betting as better hands (like a 9) will call and worse hands will mostly fold. I will check and likely call a reasonable bet if they decide to bluff.

I hold second pair (Nines) on a river board of Kc 8s 9s 3d 2d. The pot is small (400), and the action has gone check-check on both the flop and turn. My opponent (Flash) has checked to me again on the river. My hand has significant showdown value. Additionally, checking back avoids the risk of facing a check-raise, which would force me to fold a hand that likely wins at showdown against missed straight draws (JT, QJ, T7) or flush draws. Taking the free showdown is the highest EV play.

I am in the Small Blind with 9-3 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. I am on the Button (SB) with [8h 9d]. In a heads-up match with 80BB stacks, 98 offsuit is a playable hand. My previous actions show a tendency to raise with suited connectors (97s, 75s) and decent offsuit hands (QTo, K6o), while limping slightly weaker offsuit connectors (79o) and folding trash (92o, 83o). 98o is strong enough to open-raise to put pressure on the Big Blind and utilize my position post-flop, consistent with my raises in hands #22 and #32. Raising to 200 (min-raise) is the standard sizing I have been using.

I am in the Big Blind with K4o. The opponent (Pro) has min-raised to 200. With 100 more to call into a pot of 300, I am getting 3:1 odds on a call. While K4o is a weak hand, in heads-up play, it is generally strong enough to defend against a min-raise, especially since I will have the chip lead and can look to outplay the opponent post-flop or fold if I miss completely.

I have King-high on a Queen-high board. Since I am out of position and the board does not particularly favor my range after defending the Big Blind, checking is the standard and most prudent play. I will see how Pro reacts to the board before deciding whether to continue with the hand.

I hold 9-high on a Queen-high board (Qd 5s 2d) with no immediate draws. The board texture connects reasonably well with the Big Blind's defending range (Qx, flush draws, lower pairs). While I have a range advantage as the pre-flop aggressor, betting here with near-zero equity is risky if called. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, keeps the pot small, and avoids getting blown off my hand by a check-raise. It also balances my checking range to include some air alongside showdown value hands.

I am holding King-high on a board of Queen-Ten-Five-Two with two diamonds. I checked the flop and Pro checked back, suggesting they likely don't have a strong hand like a Queen. However, with no pair and no real draw, my hand has very little equity. Betting here as a bluff is possible, but given the turn card (Ten of Diamonds) has hit many marginal hands and completed potential draws, checking is the safest play. I will check to see Pro's reaction and potentially reach a cheap showdown or fold to a significant bet.

The board is now 2d 5s Qd Td. The Ten of Diamonds completes a possible flush and connects well with the Big Blind's defending range. I hold 8h 9d, giving me a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack) and a 9-high flush draw. While I have improved to a double draw, my hand has no showdown value and my draws are not to the nuts. Betting here is risky as I likely have little fold equity against a Queen, Ten, or a high diamond, and I expose myself to a check-raise which would force me to fold my equity. Checking back allows me to try to hit my straight or flush on the river for free.

I am first to act on the river with King-high. The board is [2d 5s Qd Td Qh]. Pro raised pre-flop but has checked both the flop and the turn, which typically indicates a weak range or a hand with some showdown value that doesn't want to build a large pot (like Ace-high or a small pocket pair). I have no reason to believe a bluff would be particularly effective if Pro is holding a Ten or a small pair, and I can't beat anything Pro would reasonably bet for value. I will check and hope for a free showdown, though I expect to lose to most of Pro's range.

I hold 9-high (8h 9d) on a board of [2d 5s Qd Td Qh]. The pot is 400. The action has checked through on the flop and turn. On the river, the Queen paired, and there are three diamonds on the board. My hand has zero showdown value, so I can only win by betting and inducing a fold. The opponent's line (check-call pre, check x3) is consistent with a weak made hand (like a small pair or Ace-high) or a trap. Given the board texture (paired board, possible flush), the opponent is likely to call a bluff with any pair or even high-card showdown value, suspecting I am taking a stab at the pot. Since the likelihood of getting a fold from a better hand is low and the pot is small, checking back and surrendering the pot is the variance-minimizing and likely higher EV play compared to a low-probability bluff.

Holding King-Eight offsuit on the button, I have a strong hand for heads-up play. My established strategy has been consistently raising to 300 from this position with a wide range of hands to maintain pressure and control the pot size. K8o is well above the median hand strength in this scenario, so I will continue with a standard raise to 300 chips.

I am in the Big Blind with [8h, Ah], a strong suited ace (A8s). The opponent on the Button (SB) has raised to 300 (3bb). A8s has excellent equity heads-up, dominating many Ax and Kx hands in the opponent's wide button opening range. Calling allows me to realize my equity at a good price (calling 200 to win a pot that will be 600) without bloating the pot out of position. I will call and see a flop.

I hold the Nut Flush Draw (Ah 8h) on a board of [9h, Jd, 3h]. As the pre-flop caller out of position, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. This allows me to see their action. If they bet, I have the option to check-raise as a semi-bluff with significant equity or check-call to realize that equity. If they check back, I get a free look at the turn. Donk betting here is generally less optimal than checking.

On this J-9-3 board with two hearts, my King-high has some showdown value but limited improvement potential. The opponent checked, and while a continuation bet could take the pot down now, I prefer checking back to keep the pot small and realize my equity or induce a bluff on later streets. This also protects my checking range when I miss the flop.

I hold the nut flush draw (Ah8h) on a board of [9h, Jd, 3h, Ks]. The opponent checked back the flop, capping their range, but the King on the turn connects well with broadway hands they might check back (like KQ, KJ, KT, QT). As the pre-flop caller out of position, checking is the standard play.
