I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Six offsuit. The opponent has raised to 200 from the Small Blind. Given that I have a significant stack advantage and the pot odds are 3:1 (100 to call for a total pot of 300), I will call to see a flop. While T6o is a relatively weak hand, defending the blind with these odds is standard in heads-up play, especially against a short-stacked opponent where I can use post-flop pressure.

I have middle pair (Sixes) on an Ace-high board. In heads-up play, as the Big Blind, I act first on the flop. Checking is the standard play here to see how the pre-flop raiser (Pro) reacts to this board. Given that they have many Aces in their range, I want to keep the pot small and see if they continuation bet. If they do bet, I have enough hand strength to call at least one street, but for now, checking is the best way to manage the pot and gather information.

I am on the button with K7o on an A-6-4 rainbow flop. I have a short stack (23 BBs) compared to my opponent. While the Ace hits my range well, it also hits the Big Blind's defending range. With King-high and no flush draw potential, betting turns my hand into a bluff that has very little equity if called. My opponent has shown a tendency to call flops. Checking back allows me to pot control, realize any equity I have (catching a King or Seven), and potentially win at showdown with King-high or induce bluffs on later streets. Given the dry texture and my stack situation, checking is the prudent play.

I have a pair of sixes on a board with an Ace and a Jack. Pro, the pre-flop raiser, checked back on the flop, which suggests they may not have a strong Ace. However, the Jack is another overcard to my pair. Checking here is the best play to control the size of the pot and see how Pro reacts. My hand has some showdown value, but it is not strong enough to bet for value given that Pro's checking range could still contain better pairs or draws that will call a bet.

I hold King-high (Kh 7s) on a board of 6c Ad 4h Jc. Having checked back the Ace-high flop, I have capped my range somewhat, but so has the opponent by checking twice. The Jack on the turn doesn't improve my hand, nor does it provide a strong bluffing opportunity since my flop check makes representing a strong Ace difficult. Betting now would turn my hand into a bluff with little equity if called. My King-high still has some showdown value against missed draws or weaker high cards. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, potentially hit a King or Seven on the river, or evaluate a river bet. Pot control is vital with my 23 BB stack.

I have a pair of sixes on a board with an Ace and a Jack. Pro raised pre-flop but has checked both the flop and the turn, which strongly suggests they do not have an Ace or a Jack. On the river, the five of diamonds doesn't change the board state significantly. My pair of sixes likely holds some showdown value. If I bet, I am unlikely to be called by worse hands unless Pro has a small pocket pair or a five. By checking, I allow Pro to potentially bluff with air or simply check back, allowing us to go to showdown. Given the passive line taken by Pro so far in this hand, checking is the safest way to realize my hand's equity.

On the River, the board is 6c Ad 4h Jc 5d. I hold King-high (Kh 7s). The action has been checked down on the Flop and Turn. My opponent checks to me again. The board has developed a 4-5-6 low run, making straights possible for hands like 78 or 23, though unlikely given the preflop action. My King-high has some showdown value against missed random hands or lower high cards. Betting would turn my hand into a bluff, but it is unlikely to fold out any pair (like a weak 4x or 6x) that the opponent has pot-controlled with, and worse hands will simply fold. Given my stack size (23 BB), preserving chips is priority over making a thin bluff in a small pot against a passive opponent. I will check back to realize my equity.

I am in the Small Blind/Button position with a massive chip lead over 'Pro'. My hand, 9c 4d, is weak, but in heads-up play, maintaining pressure from the button is essential. Throughout the tournament, 'Pro' has shown a tendency to fold to pre-flop raises or play fit-or-fold on the flop. By raising to 300, I am continuing my strategy of attacking his blinds and taking advantage of my positional edge to win the pot early or on the flop.

I am in the Big Blind with 93o, facing a standard 3x open (to 300) from the Button (Flash). The opponent opens this size consistently. My hand is in the bottom 10% of starting hands and has very poor playability out of position. I am getting 3:1 pot odds (calling 200 to win 600), requiring roughly 33% equity to break even on a call in terms of immediate odds, but my ability to realize that equity is low due to being out of position against an opponent who c-bets frequently. With a stack of 22BB, I cannot afford to bleed chips calling with trash hands. Reshoving is not an option. Folding is the only viable play.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with King-Ten offsuit (KTo), which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play, ranking in the top 20% of hands. With a stack of 21.5 BBs, I am in the 'reshove' territory but still deep enough to play a standard raise/fold or raise/call game rather than just open-shoving. My opponent has shown a mix of folding, calling, and 3-betting to 600 against my opens. A min-raise to 200 allows me to steal the blind if they fold, or play a pot in position with a strong hand if they call. Limping is too passive, and folding is incorrect with this hand strength.

I am in the big blind with 8c 3h, which is a weak hand. Pro has raised to 200 from the small blind. I only need to call 100 more to see a flop in a pot that would be 400 total. However, my hand has very poor playability and I have a massive chip lead (17,700 vs 2,000). While the pot odds are 3:1, I've seen Pro play fairly conservatively when I show aggression, but also capable of firing. Given the stack depths and the weakness of my hand, folding is a standard and safe play to avoid giving away chips unnecessarily.

I am in the Small Blind/Button position with 10-6 offsuit. My opponent, Pro, has a very short stack of 2200 chips (22 big blinds) while I have a massive lead. Raising to 300 has been my standard opening sizing and it puts a lot of pressure on short stacks. However, against a 22BB stack, T6o is a marginal hand. Looking at my history, I've been opening frequently and Pro has been folding often. I'll maintain aggression by raising to 300 to try and take down the blinds or force a difficult decision from the short stack.

The opponent on the Button (SB) has raised to 300 (3BB), which is their standard opening size. I am in the Big Blind with [Ks Jd] and a stack of 2200 (22 BBs). KJo is a strong hand heads-up, significantly ahead of a random hand and performs well against a standard button opening range. With pot odds of 2:1, I only need 33% equity to call, and KJo has much more than that. While a 3-bet shove is a consideration, 22 BBs is slightly deep for a standard re-steal with this holding, and I prefer to keep the opponent's range wide and realize equity post-flop rather than forcing a fold or getting called only by better hands. I will defend my blind by calling.

I have flopped top pair (Jacks) with a strong kicker (King) on a relatively dry, rainbow board [2d 7h Jc]. Being out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check. This allows the opponent to make a continuation bet with their wide button opening range, including bluffs and worse value hands (like lower pairs). Leading out (donk betting) would likely cause them to fold air hands that might otherwise bluff, and I want to keep their range as wide as possible. My hand is strong enough to call a bet or check-raise later, but for now, checking creates the most profitable scenario.

On a flop of Jc 7h 2d, I have Ten high with a backdoor straight draw. Pro checked to me. While I have no direct pair or draw, this board is generally better for the pre-flop aggressor. Pro has a very short stack of 20 big blinds (2000 chips). I will place a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to pressure Pro's marginal holdings and try to end the hand here. This has been a consistent pattern in my play that often results in folds from Pro.

I have top pair with a strong kicker (Ks Jd) on a dry, rainbow board of 2d-7h-Jc. The opponent has made a standard continuation bet of 300 into a pot of 600. With a stack of 2000 (20BB), my hand is well within the top of my range. Raising here would likely fold out the opponent's bluffs and only get action from hands that beat me (AJ, sets, overpairs) or split with me. Calling keeps the opponent's range wide, allowing them to continue bluffing on the turn or value betting worse hands (like worse Jacks or pocket pairs). The board texture is safe enough that I don't need to raise for protection. I will call to evaluate the turn and look to capture more value or induce bluffs.

I hold King-Jack offsuit on a J-7-2-5 rainbow board. I have top pair with a strong kicker. The pot is 1200 and I have 1700 remaining (SPR ~1.4). I called a c-bet on the flop. The turn card (5s) is a brick that doesn't significantly change the board texture or complete obvious draws. My opponent (Flash) has shown a tendency in previous hands to check back the turn after being called on the flop (e.g., Hands 1, 11, 19, 23, 39, 55, 59). While leading out is an option to prevent a check-back, checking is the standard play out of position to the pre-flop aggressor. It keeps the opponent's bluffing range active and allows them to value bet worse hands or bluff-shove given the stack sizes. If they check back, I can value bet the river. If they bet, I am committed to calling or jamming given my hand strength and the low SPR.

On the turn 5s, the board remains very dry. I have 10-high and missed any potential improvement. Pro checked to me again. I've already bet the flop and Pro called, which suggests they might have a piece of this board (a Jack, a Seven, or a pocket pair). However, Pro is now extremely short-stacked with only 1700 chips left. If I bet again, I might be committing myself to a shove or getting called by any pair. Given the texture and the fact that I have zero equity if called, I will check back to see a free river and potentially win if Pro is also on a weak draw or complete air, or to evaluate on the river if they lead out.

I hold Top Pair with a King kicker (Ks Jd) on a [2d 7h Jc 5s 8h] board. The opponent checked back the turn, which is a line they have taken frequently in previous hands (e.g., Hand 1, 5, 6, 32, 58, 59). In the vast majority of those instances, the opponent also checked back the river to take a free showdown with marginal made hands. Therefore, checking here would likely result in no further value extraction. To get value from worse hands such as underpairs (99, TT), middle/bottom pairs (8x, 7x), or Ace-highs that might hero call, I need to lead out. The board texture is relatively unconnected (unlike Hand 11 where they folded to a river bet on a 4-straight board), making a call more likely. A bet of roughly 40-50% pot is sized to get called by these worse holdings while maximizing value.

On the river 8h, Pro leads out for 500 into a pot of 1200. My Ten-high has virtually no showdown value, losing to any pair and most of Pro's likely bluffing range (Ace-high or King-high). Pro called my flop bet and checked the turn, which often indicates a marginal hand like a Seven or a Jack that is now looking for value or protection after I checked back the turn. Given that Pro has a very short stack and this lead looks like a value bet, there is no reason for me to make a hero call with Ten-high when I have a massive chip lead. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack advantage.

I hold Ace-Jack offsuit (Ad Jc) on the Button with a stack of 28.5 BBs. This is a premium hand in heads-up play, well ahead of the opponent's random range. My strategy is to raise to 200 (min-raise) to build the pot for value and take the initiative. Shoving is unnecessary at this depth, and limping forfeits the chance to extract value or take down the pot pre-flop. A min-raise allows me to play post-flop in position with a strong hand.

I am in the big blind with a massive chip lead. Pro has raised to 2x (200 total). I only need to call 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 3:1 pot odds. While Ten-Five offsuit is a weak hand, in heads-up play with these odds and my stack depth, it is worth seeing a flop to potentially outplay the opponent or hit a lucky board, especially since I can afford the small investment to defend my blind.

I have middle pair (fives) on a Queen-high board. While not a powerhouse, middle pair is a solid hand in heads-up play. My goal is to keep the pot manageable and see how Pro reacts. Checking allows me to see if Pro will bet or check behind, providing more information about their hand strength without committing more chips immediately.

I hold Ad Jc on a 5c 4h Qd board. I raised pre-flop and the opponent defended the Big Blind. The board is relatively dry with one high card. While I have the best hand frequently here against a random defend range, betting mainly folds out hands I crush (like K-high or air) and isolates me against pairs (Qx, 5x, 4x) or sticky draws. Checking back allows me to control the pot size with a medium-strength hand (Ace-high), realize my equity by seeing a free turn, and induce bluffs from the opponent on later streets. It also protects my checking range. Given the stack depth (27 BBs), pot control is preferred over bloating the pot with just overcards.

I have a pair of 5s on a board with two overcards (Queen and King). Pro checked back on the flop, which suggests they might not have a Queen, but the King is a new scare card. Checking here is the best way to control the pot size and move towards a cheap showdown. If Pro bets, I will have to re-evaluate based on their sizing, but for now, checking maintains the pot's current size with my marginal made hand.

I hold Ad Jc on a turn board of 5c 4h Qd Kc. The pot is 400. I raised pre-flop, and the flop was checked through. The King on the turn is a double-edged sword: it hits my perceived range as the pre-flop raiser (AK, KQ, KJ), but it also connects with the opponent's Big Blind defense range (Kx). My hand currently has some showdown value as Ace-high and I have picked up a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (need a Ten). Betting here turns my hand into a bluff, trying to fold out weak pairs like 4x or 5x, but risks being check-raised or called by Qx/Kx, forcing me to fold my equity. Checking back allows me to realize my equity for free, see the river card (potentially hitting the straight), and keeps the pot small with a marginal made hand. Given the opponent's passivity in previous hands, checking is the lower-variance play to reach showdown.

I have two pair, Kings and Fives, with a Queen kicker. The board paired on the river with a second King, which actually improves my hand from a simple pair of fives to two pair. Pro has checked both the flop and the turn, indicating a likely weak range or a marginal hand they are trying to take to showdown. Since my hand is relatively strong in a heads-up context but unlikely to get called by many worse hands if I bet, checking is the most prudent play. It allows me to catch a bluff if Pro decides to bet with air, or simply see a cheap showdown where I likely have the best hand.

I hold Ad Jc on a final board of 5c 4h Qd Kc Ks. My hand effectively plays as a Pair of Kings with an Ace kicker (K-K-A-Q-5). The action has been checked through on the flop and turn, indicating the opponent likely lacks a strong hand (Trips or strong Two Pair), though they could hold a weak pair (4x, 5x, Qx) or air. Betting here would likely fold out the hands I beat (missed draws, lower high cards) and only get called by hands that beat me (pairs) or chop (Ace-high). I have sufficient showdown value to win against pure bluffs or chop with Ax. Therefore, checking back is the optimal play to realize my equity without risking more chips in a pot where I likely have the best hand or am chopping.

I am on the button with Qs Ts, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play. My opponent has a stack of 2,600 chips (26 big blinds). I have a massive chip lead. Throughout the session, I have established a consistent strategy of raising to 300 from the button to put pressure on the big blind. This hand is well above my opening range, and I want to build a pot while maintaining my positional advantage.

I am in the Big Blind with 7c 3d, a very weak offsuit hand. My stack is 26 BBs. The opponent (Button/SB) has raised to 3x the big blind (300). I need to call 200 to win a pot of 600, requiring 33% equity. 73o has poor equity (approx 32% against a random hand) and very poor playability out of position. Facing a 3x open, I am not getting sufficient odds to defend with the bottom of my range. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities.

I hold Queen-Ten suited (Qd Td) on the Button/Small Blind, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play, well within the top range of hands. With a stack of 25.5 big blinds (2550 chips), I am in a position where I need to play aggressively to accumulate chips, but I have enough depth to play post-flop rather than just shoving pre-flop. My opponent 'Flash' has a massive chip lead and has shown a mix of folding to steals and aggressive 3-betting. A min-raise to 200 is the most standard and effective play here; it allows me to steal the blinds if he folds, or play a pot in position with a strong drawing hand if he calls. Open-shoving 25bb is a bit excessive and folds out hands I dominate while isolating me against better hands. I will raise to 200.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Seven offsuit. Pro, who is significantly short-stacked with only 24 big blinds, has raised to 200 from the button. I am being offered 3-to-1 odds to call (100 more to win a total pot of 300). Given my massive chip lead and the favorable pot odds, defending my big blind here is standard in heads-up play. I will call to see a flop and use my stack depth to navigate the rest of the hand.

On this low, coordinated flop of 5-6-3, my Jack-high hand has very little equity. Since I defended my big blind against Pro's pre-flop raise, the standard play is to check to the aggressor. This allows me to see how Pro reacts to the board and potentially realize my equity for free if they check back, or decide whether to fold if they bet, given that I have no draws or pairs.

I am on the button with QdTd. I raised pre-flop and the Big Blind called. The flop comes 5s 6s 3d. This low, connected board texture favors the Big Blind's calling range (which includes many small pairs and connected cards) more than my high-card heavy raising range. With just overcards and no flush draw (since the board is spades and I have diamonds) or significant straight draws, my hand is somewhat marginal. Betting here runs the risk of being check-raised, forcing me to fold equity, or getting called by better hands/strong draws. Checking back is the standard play for this hand class on this texture to exercise pot control, realize equity for free, and potentially call a bet on the turn if I improve or the card is favorable.

The turn 9d provides me with a double gutshot straight draw (outs are 4s and 8s). Pro checked back the flop, which suggests a range of weak made hands or Ace-high. By checking again, I am looking to realize my equity for free or potentially see how Pro values their hand if they decide to bet. Given my significant stack lead, I prefer to keep the pot small while I'm still drawing.

I turned a diamond flush draw (Qd Td on 5s 6s 3d 9d) to go with my two overcards. While betting has merit to fold out better high cards (like King-high) or deny equity to random hands, checking back is the superior option given my stack size (24bb). Betting opens me up to a check-raise, which would force me to fold my significant equity or commit too many chips on a draw. Checking guarantees I see the river card to try and hit my flush or top pair. If I improve, I can bet for value on the river; if I miss, I can re-evaluate. Pot control and equity realization are the priorities here.

I have Jack-high with no showdown value on a board of 5-6-3-9-3. Pro raised pre-flop but has checked both the flop and the turn, which caps their range to weak made hands or missed overcards. While I could attempt to bluff here to take down the small pot, Jack-high is unlikely to win at showdown if I check. However, Pro's passive line suggests they might be willing to call a small bet with Ace-high or a small pair. Given my significant chip lead and the fact that I have no blockers to Pro's folding range, I will check and concede the pot rather than risking more chips on a low-equity bluff.

I arrived at the river with Queen-high (missed flush draw and overcards) on a board of 5s 6s 3d 9d 3h. The action went check-check on both the flop and turn. My opponent (Flash) has checked the river as well. The pot is small (400 chips). My hand has some showdown value against missed spade draws (like JsTs, 8s7s) or random lower cards, but loses to King-high, Ace-high, and any pair. Betting here is risky because my line (check-check-bet) looks polarized/bluffy, and Flash, having a massive chip lead, might look me up with Ace-high or a small pair (5x, 6x) that checked down. Betting likely only folds out hands that I already beat or chops, while getting called by everything that beats me. With a stack of 24bb, preserving chips is more important than attempting a thin bluff in a small pot. I will take the free showdown.

I am on the button with a massive chip lead over Pro (27 BBs). Q3o is a marginal hand, but in heads-up play, maintaining aggression from the button is key, especially since Pro has been folding frequently to my 3x pre-flop raises in recent hands. I will raise to 300 to apply pressure and attempt to take the blinds immediately.

I am in the Big Blind with Q9o facing a 3x raise from the Button (Flash). My stack is 2700 (27 BB). I need to call 200 to win a pot that will be 600, giving me 33% pot odds. Q9o is a strong enough hand heads-up to defend against a wide button opening range, as it has approximately 47-50% equity against a typical 50% opening range. Shoving 27bb is excessive, and folding is too tight given the price. Calling allows me to see a flop with a playable hand.

I have Queen-high on an Ace-Jack-Four board with two spades. This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range, which includes many Ax and broadway hands. I have no pair, no flush draw, and minimal straight potential. Being out of position with a very weak hand, checking is the only standard option. I intend to fold to a continuation bet.

I hold Queen-high on an Ace-high board with a Jack and two spades. As the pre-flop aggressor, this board texture strongly favors my range, as I am more likely to have strong Aces (AK, AQ, AJ) and pocket pairs. Pro checked to me, which is standard. By continuation-betting half the pot (300 chips), I can put pressure on Pro's marginal holdings like low pocket pairs or middle-card misses that might have called pre-flop. If Pro has an Ace or a strong Jack, they will likely call, but a bet here allows me to take down the pot immediately when they have missed, which aligns with my goal of accumulating chips from my position of strength.

On a Flop of Js Ac 4s, I hold Qh 9s. I have Queen-high with no pair and no immediate draw, although I hold one spade. The board texture heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor who has all the strong Aces and broadway combinations in their range. Facing a half-pot continuation bet (300 into 600), my hand has very little equity and no path to realizing it out of position. As planned in the previous street's analysis, I will fold to the bet to preserve my 25 BB stack for better opportunities.

I am playing heads-up on the button with 24.5 BBs, a significant disadvantage against the chip leader. My hand, J7o, is marginal—often an open in a vacuum but near the bottom of the playable range. Reviewing recent history, the opponent 'Flash' has been aggressively 3-betting my button raises (e.g., Hands 26, 44, 74, 78), forcing me to fold and bleed chips. J7o is not strong enough to call a 3-bet or 4-bet shove. Opening this hand invites a high-variance spot where I am likely to be blown off my equity or forced to play a bloated pot with a weak hand. Preserving my stack for better spots where I can withstand aggression or have higher equity is the prudent adjustment here. Therefore, I will fold.

I am holding 8d 4s on the button. While this is a weak hand, I have a massive chip lead over my opponent, Pro, who has approximately 23.5 big blinds remaining. Based on previous hands, Pro has been playing relatively tight and folding frequently to pre-flop aggression. I will continue my strategy of applying pressure with a standard 3x raise to 300, aiming to pick up the blinds and the small pot immediately. If Pro moves all-in, I can easily fold with minimal loss.

I am in the Big Blind with Q5o and a short stack of 23.5 BBs. The opponent has raised to 3 BBs (300). Although I get 3:1 pot odds requiring ~25% equity, and Q5o has raw equity around 38-40% against a wide button range, playing this hand out of position with a shallow stack is difficult. The hand has poor playability and reverse implied odds (often dominated if I hit top pair). Against a 3x open size which applies more pressure than a min-raise, and given my need to preserve chips for better shoving or defending opportunities to close the massive stack gap, folding is the disciplined choice.

I am on the button with 23 big blinds, holding a suited two-gapper (9s6s). This hand has reasonable post-flop playability due to straight and flush potential. In heads-up play, the button range should be wide (often 80%+), and suited connectors/gappers are standard opens. Although I am the short stack and vulnerable to 3-bets from the chip leader, playing passively (limping or folding) forfeits the initiative and the chance to take the blinds uncontested. I have raised similar hands (like 75s in Hand #10) previously. A min-raise to 200 gives me the best balance of risk and potential reward, allowing me to play the hand in position if called.

I have been dealt the best possible starting hand, pocket Aces. My opponent (Pro) has a very short stack of 2,150 chips remaining. My goal here is to extract as much value as possible or simply get the money in while I am a massive favorite. Pro has opened for 200. If I just call, I might let them see a cheap flop, but with their stack size, I want to build a pot immediately. A 3-bet to 600 or 700 is standard, but given the shallow stacks, I will raise to 600 to induce a shove or commit them to the pot.

I am facing a 3-bet to 600 from the Big Blind after opening to 200. I need to call 400 chips into a pot that would be 1200. My remaining stack would be 1550, creating a very low SPR of roughly 1.3. While 9s6s is suited and has some connectivity, it is significantly dominated by a typical 3-betting range. In previous hands (e.g., #26, #44, #74, #78), I have folded better or similar hands (like K6s, Q6s, T9o) to 3-bets from this opponent. Calling commits nearly 30% of my stack pre-flop with a speculative hand that rarely flops strong enough to commit the rest. Shoving is too risky given the likelihood of being called by the chip leader. Preserving my ~20BB stack for a better spot is the optimal strategy.

I am on the button with Q5o. My opponent, Pro, is relatively short-stacked with about 20 big blinds. Looking at the previous hand history, Pro has been playing extremely tight, even folding premium hands to pre-flop aggression. A standard raise to 300 chips from the small blind is a high-frequency play in heads-up to pressure a short stack and capitalize on their tendency to fold. I will maintain my aggressive image and raise.

I am in the Big Blind with [5d, 8d] and a stack of 20.5 BB. The opponent (Flash) has a massive chip lead and has raised to 300 (3 BB). While 8d5d is suited, it is a weak hand (three-gapper). Pot odds require 33% equity to call, but being out of position against an aggressive big stack significantly reduces my equity realization. Calling 10% of my stack pre-flop with a speculative hand against a 3x open is risky and likely -EV in the long run given my short stack. A 20 BB stack allows me to wait for a better spot to shove or defend. I choose to fold and preserve my chips.

I am in the Small Blind with 20 BBs (2000 chips). My opponent, Flash, has a massive chip lead and has been defending his Big Blind aggressively, frequently 3-betting my recent opens (e.g., Hands #92, #78, #74). Q4s is a marginal hand; while suited, the kicker is weak. Raising would commit 10% of my stack and force a fold against a 3-bet, which is a likely outcome given recent history. Open-limping is an alternative but risks facing a raise which I would have to fold to, wasting chips. Given the stack depth and the opponent's aggression, preserving chips for a better spot to shove or raise for value is the prudent play. I will fold.

I am in the Small Blind/Button with 2d 4h, which is a very weak hand. The pot is currently 150 and it costs 50 to call. My opponent, Pro, has a very short stack of 1900 compared to my 17950. While I could attempt a steal by raising, 2d 4h is near the bottom of my range and Pro has been folding to some of my raises but also occasionally 3-betting or calling and showing down. Given the extreme stack disparity and the weakness of my hand, there is little incentive to play this hand. Folding is the safest and most standard play to avoid giving the short stack any momentum.

I am on the Button (SB) with a stack of 20 BBs holding Ten-Deuce offsuit (T2o), which is one of the weakest hands in Hold'em. Despite having position post-flop in heads-up play, the hand has very poor equity and playability. My stack is getting short (2000 chips), so I cannot afford to bleed chips by opening with trash hands or limping only to face aggression from the Big Blind who covers me significantly. The standard and most profitable play here is to simply fold and wait for a better hand.

I am currently holding 4c 2d, which is a very weak hand. My opponent is short-stacked with only 1,900 chips (19 big blinds). While I have a massive chip lead, there is no strategic reason to enter a pot with such a poor hand and risk giving the opponent a double-up opportunity or unnecessary momentum. I will fold and wait for a more profitable spot.

I am on the Button (Small Blind) with 9d 3d and a stack of 20 BBs (2000 chips). My opponent has a significant chip lead. 93s is a weak hand, below the median strength for heads-up play. Given my short stack, I need to preserve chips for better opportunities to shove or raise for value. Recent history shows I have been folding my small blind frequently, but this hand does not justify deviating from a tight strategy to steal, as the risk of getting 3-bet or called by a better hand is high. Limping is an option, but folding is safer to avoid bleeding chips in a disadvantageous spot.
